turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did it say prohibit; it just said regulate, did it not?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: The —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, never mind, maybe I have it wrong.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Well, footnote two reads as follows, "Because the Court of Appeals concluded that the labor dispute exception was not severable from the remainder of the statute, it invalidated the enactment in its entirety. The Court therefore found it unnecessary to consider the constitutionality under the First Amendment of the statute that prohibited all residential picketing. Because find the present statute defective on equal protection principles, we likewise do not consider whether a statute barring all residential picketing regardless of its subject matter would violate the First and Fourteenth Amendments." And so that situation then is now a situation that is squarely before the Court today. Because we do have an ordinance that prohibits all residential picketing without the labor exception. The fact situation that gave rise to the adoption of these two ordinances was a period of picketing of approximately three weeks that had been conducted by the Appellees and their associates before the home of one Dr. Benjamin Victoria, a resident of the Town of Brookfield. The purpose of the picketing was to denounce him as an abortionist and as a killer of babies. The town board had no desire to get involved in this debate. However, it was appalled at the invasion of privacy that occurred, and also it was appalled at the very serious --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now this picketing was at the Doctor's home, was it not?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: It was at the Doctor's home.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He did not operate a clinic, did he, from his home?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: No, he did it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was just where he lived?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: That was just where he lived. In fact, he had two clinics, one in Milwaukee, and one in another city, but not in his home.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: None in Brookfield?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: None in the Town of Brookfield, right. So in oral argument today, I propose to concentrate on three principal points. The first is that the District Court order was filed, and it was a final order. Second, that the residential streets of the Town of Brookfield are not public fora. And third, that assuming arguendo that the streets of the Town of Brookfield are public fora, that nevertheless that the ordinance is a valid and constitutional regulation of time, place and manner, and to achieve two important governmental interests, the one being residential privacy, and the other being public safety.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fuhrman, we are reviewing, I guess, the preliminary injunction. So is our standard of review whether the lower court abused its discretion in the issuance of the injunction?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: We contend that it is not. And I would like to point out perhaps at this point that although the order of the District Court is in form a preliminary injunction, actually however, it has finally decided the constitutional issue involved, at least from the standpoint of the District Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You still want to go to trial?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: No. Let me explain. The preliminary injunction so-called said that it would become final in the event that there were no requests for a trial, number one, by any party; and number two, that an appeal would be taken. So the protect the interests of the Town of Brookfield, we did appeal and we did file a request for a trial. But it is clear from a reading of the order that the Court had already determined that it would be unnecessary to have an evidentiary hearing. And there were two reasons for that. Number one, it was stipulated by both parties that an evidentiary hearing was not necessary. And number two, the way in which this ordinance was attacked by the Plaintiffs was not in a factual context at all. They attacked it facially before it ever had an opportunity to be enforced. Shortly after the ordinance was adopted, there was a request for the preliminary injunction. And so this particular ordinance has yet to be enforced. And so there is no factual context within which the District Court could construe the ordinance. It is purely a matter of law. If there were to be a trial, the trial would be purely on that constitutional issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fuhrman, can I ask whether this ordinance would prohibit someone picketing generally, that is suppose a picket was not stationed in front of this Doctor's house, but would a group of people be able to parade through the neighborhood carrying signs and just leave?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What does picketing consist of under the ordinance?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Although the ordinance contains no definition section, in our opinion, picketing would be having the picket proceed on a definite course or route in front of a home. Because keep in mind, the ordinance only prohibits picketing before and about a dwelling or a residence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that means that you have to be concentrating on one dwelling?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If you were just walking through the neighborhood, that would not qualify?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: That would not qualify.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose you were walking by a home with a picket sign that said, "I am against the war in the Asian Gulf", would that be a violation of the statute?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: In the Town of Brookfield, under this ordinance, it would be.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what would that damage be to the city?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: The damage would be to privacy. Because the ordinance is content neutral. It has nothing to do with the message at all.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So if you had some people marching up and down these streets saying vote for so and so for sheriff, that would be banned, too?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: If as Justice Scalia asked, it was a march, the answer would be that it would not be in violation of the ordinance. However, if the pickets actually pursued a route only in front of this particular house, any particular house which is targeted, then it would be a violation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do the findings of fact indicate that there is a commercial area or business area in which picketing can take place, was that a stipulation or a finding?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: It is an uncontested fact, yes. There are two commercial areas in the Town of Brookfield along the Bluemound Road.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Near this residence, is the commercial area anywhere near this residence?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is the Doctor's clinic in Brookfield or is it elsewhere?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: It is elsewhere.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How far away is the nearest commercial area on Bluemound Road from this residence?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: I would estimate approximately three miles.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is the width of the street there?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: The width of the residential streets in the Town of Brookfield are thirty feet.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Any sidewalks?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: And there are no sidewalks, and no curb and gutter. And this, of course, brings you into focusing upon the public safety aspect. And I should point out that in our brief that we incorporated by reference the opinion of Judge Coffey of the Seventh Circuit, and it was his dissenting opinion. And the reason that we did that was because among other things, he has in his opinion a very good analysis of the safety aspect of this case, and it is printed in the joint appendix. And if it has not been read by any member of the Court, I would urge its being read, because I think that it is very helpful to understand the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We have no record as to the conduct of the pickets; there was picketing, was there not?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: There was picketing. And there is a record of its conduct. The picketing occurred, however, prior to the adoption of the ordinance. And the reason why that record exists is because the picketers had to show their standing to bring the action. And they, of course, had been picketing right up until the time that the legislation was adopted. So when the legislation was adopted, they then commenced their action and applied for the preliminary injunction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what was their conduct?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Well, there were pickets of eleven to forty people picketing off and on over a period of approximately three weeks. Their conduct was to denounce the Doctor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How, vocally or signs?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Both, both vocally and by sign. He was described as a baby killer, and there were other picturesque phrases that are in the record.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Stop abortion, and abortion is murder?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Yes, among other things. Yes, Justice Marshall.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Here you are not complaining about that?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: No, we are not complaining about that. We are complaining about two things. The invasion of the privacy of this residence, and also the threat to public safety.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You are still relying on the threat to public safety?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Yes, we are.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Although you just told me that there would be no problem if the picketers kept moving throughout the whole neighborhood, I mean it is less of a threat if it occurs in the whole neighborhood than if it occurs in front of one house?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: As we pointed out in our brief, in the event that you have a parade rather than simply a picket, you do have a less hazardous situation. We concede, first of all, that any walking on the street by pedestrians is dangerous. But it is more dangerous if you have a picket line. Because you have these people not actually in continuing movement, but you have them moving only a short distance, and then following their picket route, you see.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You will forgive me if I do not find that self-evident. I think that it surely depends on how people are walking and what they are doing. And you could be walking the same way throughout the whole neighborhood, two abreast or back and forth or whatever. It seems to me that if you are focusing on the fact that it is in front of one house, that it must have something to do with matters other than safety.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, your record certainly contains instances of interference, does it not?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Well, the record contains instances of interference, which of course goes beyond picketing, such as obstructing the driveway and trespassing upon property. Now as has been pointed out by the Appellees, there are ordinances addressed to those particular problems. The point that we are discussing today really is the picketing ordinance itself.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fuhrman, would the town prohibit Appellees from standing in the street in front of the house and handing out leaflets containing the same messages as on the signs?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: No. It is our understanding that leafleting is not covered by this ordinance. Now I could conceive of a situation where a picketer might have leaflets and do two different things. But leafleting is not picketing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But what is a picket, does a picket have to have a sign?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: He does not have to have a sign.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose he just stands there handing our leaflets, but he stands right at the one stop, is that picketing?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: If he is following a picket route, he is picketing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I will give you the facts. The lot is sixty feet. And just in order to amuse himself, he goes up on the sixty feet until somebody comes out, and then he gives him the leaflet.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: All right. What I am saying is that he is performing two functions. One, he is picketing; and one, he is leafleting.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose he just stands in one spot and hands out the leaflet, is that a picket?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Not a picket.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if he moves ten feet of the way, then that is a picket?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: If he is moving and following a picket route, he is a picket, right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even if he is not carrying a sign?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: That is true. For instance, although I am not aware of any Appellate Court decisions on this, there have been instances where a member of the Ku Klux Klan has picketed from one point to another point following a picket route in a residential area. And that would be a violation of the ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let us assume that he was in a black neighborhood and he walked up and down a whole block and did not concentrate on one house, is that picketing?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Then it would not be picketing within the meaning of our ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he is concentrating on all of the houses there, all of the houses.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: I understand what you are saying. And I am simply drawing a distinction between picketing and parade

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fuhrman, do you say that a single picketer, let us say present one hour a day, in front of a residence substantially interferes with the residential privacy?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: I certainly do. Let us just take the Ku Klux Klan picketer that I just mentioned before. Certainly one picket making that route back and forth in front of one home that he is targeting would violate the privacy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For one hour a day or half an hour a day?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Yes, ma'am. I would say so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or once a week?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: I would say that if he did it once a week, that it would also be a violation of the privacy of that home.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Sufficiently substantial to justify the First Amendment infringement?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: It certainly would. The second point that we planned to discuss was the matter of the streets of Brookfield not being a public fora. Now we realize, of course, that there are cases that have substantial dicta indicating a repetition of the cliche that streets are quintessential public fora since time immemorial. And we believe that the key word in that cliche is the word immemorial. We realize that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: One man's cliche is another man's fundamental principle. Be careful here.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Touche. Nevertheless, I think that we have to really realize the historical origin of this. Many, many years ago, the streets of Ancient Rome converged upon a public square known as the Forum. And all of the principal buildings were at the Forum, and that Forum was certainly the place of discussion and of debate. And all other forums have derived their names from that location. We submit, however, that in 20th Century United States that to say that every street is a public forum is simply a statement contrary to fact. Take for instance, the major arteries of the interstate highway system. Here you have a system of arteries that penetrate all of the major cities of the United States. And nevertheless, they are — intent, because of the neighbor consensus.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But is it not normal that in a lot of neighborhoods like that that you do in fact walk along the edge of the road, do you not?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: This does happen.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So I would think that the drivers would be aware of the fact that it is not a heavily trafficked area, I am sure, and they would know that you have to drive rather carefully. What is the speed limit, about fifteen miles an hour?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: No, it is twenty miles an hour.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Twenty miles an hour. So it is not high traffic and high speed driving.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: No. And of course, I would concede that any pedestrian on these streets is to some extent --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That sounds like the ordinance is enacted for the protection of the picketer.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Well, this is the reason why I am suggesting reading the dissenting opinion of Judge Coffey. Because he not only talks about the protection of the picketer, but he also discusses the responsibility of the municipality for the safety of all people on the public highways.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: This is quite a separate argument from frankly what to me is more persuasive, the interest of the resident in not having someone who is arguably hostile out in front all day long. That is the interest frankly that would concern me more than worrying about whether the fellow would step in front of fifteen mile an hour school bus or something like that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that is the interest that Carey spoke about, Carey v. Brown.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Well, Carey v. Brown primarily dealt with the matter of privacy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: And in fact —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why do you not talk about that. I really think a whole lot of us want to hear about this.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: We believe that this Court should follow its own precedent that it established in the City of Renton case, and protect residential privacies. In Carey v. Brown, this Court stated, "Preserving the sanctity of the home, the one retreat to which men and women can repair to escape from the tribulations of their daily pursuits, is surely an important issue. Our decisions reflect no lack of solicitude for the right of an individual to be let alone in the privacy of a home, sometimes the last citadel of the tired, the weary and the sick."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fuhrman, do you think that the First Amendment under that precedent would enable a city to prevent street could be subject to an ordinance like this?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: The rule that we believe that the Court should follow is the rule of Cornelius v. NAACP. We believe that like other publicly owned property, that each street should be determined as to whether or not it lends itself to the First Amendment activity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If the focus of the ordinance is an individual home, what difference would it make what kind of street it is, as long as the picketing is aimed at one home on a particular street, as the invasion of privacy is just as great?

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: I agree. But we have in our argument really two propositions. Number one, we are arguing that streets, the residential streets of Brookfield, are not public fora. And the reason that we are making that argument is because that establishes a different standard for evaluating the ordinance. But we are arguing, number two, that in the event that this Court should find that notwithstanding everything that I have said that all streets without exception are public fora, that then in that event that the ordinance is still constitutional as a valid time, place and manner, regulation of two important governmental interests.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It is going to take a lot of litigation to litigate every street in the country to figure out whether they are public fora or not.

turns.name: FUHRMAN
turns.text: Well, if you had litigation on each and every street, yes. But on the other hand, we believe that reason would enter in here. Because if you look at the streets of the Town of Brookfield, and you know that they are thirty feet in width, you should know that you do not have public fora.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Fuhrman. We will hear now from you, Mr. McDowell. ORAL ARGUMENT BY STEVEN FREDERICK MCDOWELL, ESQ.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Before this Court today is an attempted appeal from an order affirming a preliminary injunction. There is no finality, no ultimate determination on the merits of the constitutional issue, and therefore no appeal jurisdiction. In light of the preliminary nature of the case, this case may well be more appropriately handled by way of summary affirmance rather than a comprehensive review of the town's claims, claims which we contend lack support in either the record nor the decisions of this Court. The town's case rests fundamentally upon a challenge of two basic premises of First Amendment law. That streets are quintessential public fora; and that picketing, a legitimate peaceful forum of First Amendment expression is to be permitted in such public fora areas. The District Court, we contend and we believe, did not abuse its discretion in determining that the picketers were likely to prevail on the merits of their First Amendment claims.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The District Court or Court of Appeals, either one, Mr. McDowell, indicate that they thought more facts were necessary in order for them to reach a conclusion on the constitutional issue?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The District Court's opinion indicated that based upon the facts that are currently in the record that there were sufficient facts to grant the preliminary injunction and in fact to grant a final injunction. We believe that this is in fact an appropriate determination. Because based upon the normal assumptions that are normally made about streets, that they are normally public fora, and also based upon the normal conclusion that picketing is a legitimate form of expression, just based upon the facts as developed there, that there would be a sufficient record to make that determination. However, if other facts are sought to be introduced, such as for example, the question of whether one must move or remain stationary to constitute picketing in the Town of Brookfield, perhaps it may be necessary to go back for a further determination.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the town says that it does not have anything else to offer.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is correct. And if that be the case, then it would seem that this Court may well wish to go ahead with a final decision on the merits. However, we would point out that there are a number of facts that the town has asserted in its brief, such as the lack of past use of the streets of the Town of Brookfield for picketing, which are not currently in the record. So if this Court wishes to rely on such facts, it may be necessary for a further determination. But based upon the facts now in the record, there is in fact evidence sufficient to support a preliminary injunction in favor of the picketers in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if the town wants the issue decided on the present record, does that make it a final judgment?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: I am not certain of that fact, because I am not certain of that point. Normally, a preliminary injunction is considered a non-final order. And secondly, there has not been a square holding on the constitutional issue as of yet. Because all that we have at this point is a preliminary injunction. A final injunction was never issued. The town could have simply allowed a final injunction to be issued, in which case there would be no problem with finality in this case. It chose not to do so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did they not ask for a trial? I thought that they asked for a trial.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: They did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So they obviously are not satisfied with the record.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Well, that is the way that the picketers have to construe the status of the case at this point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But before us today, I guess that Mr. Fuhrman says that he does not now want a trial. Is that what you heard?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is what I heard. So that is something that is Mr. Fuhrman's decision to make, and not the decision of the picketers, since we did not request a trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The problem here is not finality at all, I do not think, under the provisions of the statute. An order granting a preliminary injunction is appealable. The only question is what standard of review do you use in judging whether or not the preliminary injunction was correctly granted. Everybody concedes that there was at least a preliminary injunction. That is appealable to the Seventh Circuit, and we have jurisdiction to review a case that is in the Seventh Circuit.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Yes, it would be reviewed on the basis of an abuse of discretion standard in such a situation. However, there is a question of whether there is a square holding on the constitutional point. Because the lower court judge utilized the normal standard that is used in the Seventh Circuit, which is the standard of whether one is likely to prevail upon the merits.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You were just talking about appellate jurisdiction, were you not, not certiorari jurisdiction?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is correct. We were speaking of appellate jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So if you are talking about jurisdiction, we can always just grant cert.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Oh, certainly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that would still leave the standard of review that the Chief Justice was talking about.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is correct. And in fact, the District Court did not abuse its discretion in granting the preliminary injunction in this case. When we turn to the substance of the case and the merits of the case on the free speech issue —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. McDowell, is this a facial attack on the ordinance?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The attack is more in line of a facial attack, in that what the picketers are challenging is the application of the ordinance to picketers in general and into In this case, the town in its brief indicated that District Court and at the Seventh Circuit. And in this case, there may well be that the ordinance sweeps substantially overbroadly with respect to protected expression.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If you can only conceive of one instance in which it is okay and only one, one might suspect that it is overbroad.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is absolutely correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I would think so.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I must confess that I am a little puzzled. Your complaint suggests that you are concerned about your client's own right to picket.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because they have been threatened with arrest. But the District Court found what kind of picketing that they had engaged in the past, namely eleven to forty people, and there were some unfortunate remarks and one thing or another of that kind. Should we ask the question whether that particular type of picketing is constitutionally protected?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That type of picketing serves as a background certainly that this Court can consider and can consider in terms of the motivation perhaps of the town in passing this ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if we were to assume that that is all that it really prohibits, and that is all that you really care about, because you want to engage in exactly what you have done before, and then focus on that issue instead of all of these hypothetical things like leafleting. Would you say that you could picket with forty persons out in front all day long seven days a week in front of this house?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The problem is that the ordinance forbids more than simply that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, first answer my question, would you.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: We do not believe that the picketers have an absolute right to picket without respect to considerations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think that they have a constitutional right to engage in the kind of picketing that the District Court found in this case?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Yes, I do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You do. Forty persons seven days a week right straight through. And if that is true and if we are focusing on this picketing, what was the audience at which this picketing was directed?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The picketing was directed at both Dr. Victoria and his neighbors.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see, thank you.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is what the affidavits show and the record shows in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What do you mean directed at, was it in front of the neighbors' houses or in front of his house?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: It was in front of his house.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that is generally referred to as picketing him, not picketing his neighbors.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is correct. The question I believe that Justice Stevens has was a question as to who the proper audience was. Certainly, neighbors going back and forth would be able to see the signs, and obviously would be an audience.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. McDowell, you mentioned a couple of basic principles of First Amendment law when you started off. On another one, and we just issued an opinion today that averts to it indirectly, is that picketing is different from other First Amendment activities. It can be intimidating. It is different from leafleting, and it is different from pure speech. As far as the merely informative content of letting the neighbors know that this man is an abortionist and that you disapprove of it, as far as that is concerned, you would leaflet, you could slip information under the doors of the neighbors, and you could march around the whole neighborhood with a sign, as counsel for the city says is permissible under this ordinance. But you do not want to do that. You want to essentially hassle this doctor, to put it in the vernacular. You want to be in front of his house and bring home to him your displeasure with him in, maybe intimidating is not the word, but an annoying fashion essentially. Is that not what is going on, is there any other reason why you have to picket his house?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: We do not claim a right to harass. And in this case, that is not what was involved. Because public issue picketing is in many ways quite similar to leafleting. Because what is involved is the transmission of a message, in this case a message of opposition to abortion. Picketing was a means utilized. However, what was involved here was not the type of perhaps signal that one might have in a labor picketing instance, but instead merely a use of the signs and the picketing to indicate disapproval.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When you picket a store, that is a logical place to do that, because you are sending your message to the customers of that store or a business. Or you are picketing a plant that is on strike. You are sending the message to the other workers who might want to come in. To whom are you sending the message here, that they could not get it anywhere else, why is this a logical place to get this message across to anyone except the doctor whose privacy you are invading?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Well, it is a legitimate place, as the picketers noted in their affidavits, to transmit their message both to the doctor and also to his neighbors.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You can get it to his neighbors very easily. His neighbors do not congregate in front of his house.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The reason why picketing may be a particularly sensible type of procedure by which to transmit such a message is that a picket sign is a sign that a neighbor or anyone going by can look at and notice what the opinions are of the picketers. It is in essence something like a mass media that can be done at a minimum amount of expense to the picketers involved.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You can do that walking around the neighborhood. In fact, it would be better. You would not have to rely on the neighbors happening to come out by the house. You do not want to do that. You want to stand right in front of his house. You want to stick your thumb in his eye essentially. Is that not what it is about, the annoying nature of picketing?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: I do not believe that it is in this case. Because the picketers' desire is a desire simply to transmit their message involved, and they wish to utilize picketing which has been recognized as a legitimate First Amendment protected means of expression.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why not picket him at his clinic?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You can go up to Appleton or into Milwaukee and picket him at his clinic.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: It is possible to picket at other locations. However, the mere fact that one can picket at another location does not permit a picketing ban in a certain location unless such a ban is narrowly tailored to support specific interests, either in safety or in residential privacy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I think that any court familiar with our cases might be more likely to uphold a ban on residential picketing if there was an opportunity to picket the individual that they desire to bring the message home to somewhere else.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Certainly, alternative channels can figure into the analysis. However, once again, the key point is is this ordinance which forbids all picketing flatly in the Town of Brookfield an ordinance which is narrowly tailored to support the interests involved.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why do you say that it is not narrowly tailored to support privacy and safety?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Turning first to safety, the reason why it is not narrowly tailored to support the safety interests is number one, because the ordinance prohibits only picketing but permits a number of expressive and non-expressive activities which can have just as severe an effect upon the interests of free vehicular movement and free pedestrian movement and the like as anything else. Furthermore, the ordinance addresses the problem in a way which does not make much sense. If the problem is with the picketers' safety and with the safety of the community with concerns about traffic, what is done is that the picketers are moved off of a property where perhaps there is less traffic and moved on to Bluemound Road, a busy highway. Where seemingly if one is concerned about safety, and traffic, and the like, that the distractions will be greater than in the residential neighborhood.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As to privacy?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: As to privacy, the reason --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How do you tailor this more narrowly than an absolute prohibition?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Well, a number of different options are available to the town. First, with respect to the adverse effects of picketing, the town has a number of ordinances currently in effect, ordinances banning such things as littering, obstruction of the streets and so forth, which apply equally to expressive and non-expressive activity, which could

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For example, they have an ordinance or law of some kind under which the picketers for crowding and such numbers on the lawn could be arrested?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Well, there is a trespass ordinance which would apply to such activity on the lawn.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could they be arrested for violation of that ordinance?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is correct. They could, if they were on the lawn, under the construction of the state constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any way that they could not be on the lawn?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: They would be on the streets. And there, you have the question of whether such activity would obstruct the street. And there, what one could do perhaps, and the town has not done this -- the point is that the town has banned all picketing irrespective of numbers -- the town could perhaps place some limitation on the numbers of picketers, some limitation on the time at which the picketers could be out there, but it has not done that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let me interrupt you. You told me a minute ago that this picketing of forty persons all day long was constitutionally protected. How could they put a limit on, you mean no more than fifty would be the limit, or could they put a limit on no more than one or two?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: We believe, and obviously this is a legislative question which the town would have to address, and it would present a different constitutional question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It is not a legislative question. You are telling us what they could constitutionally do, that they constitutionally could do these alternative things, all of which are different from what you a few minutes ago told me that they could not constitutionally do.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Let me clarify then my statement of a few minutes ago. The basic protection of picketing is something that is constitutionally protected. Certainly, numbers of picketers could be regulated to the extent that numbers of picketers interfere either with free use of the streets or with the interests of the town in privacy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, specifically, could they pass an ordinance saying that you may not picket with more than ten persons in front of a house for more than eight hours a day?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: We would have with respect to that ordinance the similar analysis that would have to be applied. That is does the ordinance restrict picketing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand the analysis. I am curious to know what your answer is.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: All right. My answer would be that ten picketers would raise a severe question, because there would be a question of whether a limitation --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand that there is a severe question, but what is the answer to it?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Okay. I would say that that would not be constitutional, because there would not be a significant enough impact upon public safety interests to justify the ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In other words, you gave me the same answer that you did before, the picketing that you engaged in, you think was constitutionally protected. And therefore, these alternatives about changing numbers or hours are purely just argument. You do not really believe that.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The question with respect to whether the picketers would believe that or not, I think would largely be determined if the town passed such an ordinance, would the picketers then challenge it as an unreasonable limitation on their speech. And that is a question for a future case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it is not so much the picketers. You are giving answers saying that although the town cannot do what it has done, that it could do these other things. But then that seems inconsistent with the answer that you think that the Constitution protects the way that you actually picketed here, which would be prohibited by some of the alternatives that you propose. So the question is how serious you are about the alternatives.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: I would answer the question this way. We are serious about the alternatives. It is possible that some of the picketers' activities might have violated the Constitution under a narrowly tailored ordinance. This ordinance, however, is not narrowly tailored.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose that we thought that as applied to the picketing that actually had taken place and presumably would take place again, that the ordinance was quite constitutional, what if we thought that. Should we not then just sustain the ordinance, or what else do you have to offer?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: I would again go back to points that have been emphasized with respect to the entire discussion of this case. What is not involved here is an enforcement action on picketing which took place after the ordinance was passed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know. But if this is a facial attack, and suppose we say that as applied to this picketing as applied by the District Court, that this ordinance is quite constitutional. Now is not your only rejoinder then, well, that may be so, but it is overbroad?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is right. That is right. If our activity is not protected, then it becomes an overbreadth challenge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then what do we have to do, do we have to imagine the reach of the ordinance. It may not be substantially overbroad.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: That is perhaps a question that would then have to be determined at trial. There might have to be additional facts and so forth put out as to the enforcement policies of the town, which might need to be dealt with at that point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that may be. But to do that, we should just reverse, we should reverse the Court of Appeals?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: No, but --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We could never decide an overbreadth case if we had to do that, to go back to see exactly how the statute would be applied in these situations. That is not how we do overbreadth.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Overbreadth is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If it is overbroad, it is overbroad, is it not?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Right. Overbreadth is basically a question of applying the sweep of the statute, and in essence determining that the statute can apply very clearly to protected activity. And that is apparent with respect to this ordinance which bans all residential picketing in a neighborhood without any concerns.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Have we ever applied overbreadth to an ordinance or a statute dealing just with picketing?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: I believe that the Thornhill case, which overbreadth was first utilized, was a picketing case. So picketing cases are subject to overbreadth analysis.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Have you ever checked on how many times Thornhill has been cited?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: I have not checked out how many times, but I believe with some degree of frequency in my reading of this Court's First Amendment cases in preparation here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In your answer to Justice Stevens, you indicated that privacy is a protectable interest insofar as the state is concerned, the privacy of homes?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Privacy of homes is a protectable interest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Can it be protected from picketing in any degree?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: In applying such an analysis, what has to be determined is what precisely is the character of the privacy interest. For example, in the Keith case, this Court seemed to indicate that there were questions with respect to the application of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you have a right to a quiet street?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Excuse me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there a right to a quiet street?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The residential privacy interests can extend to some extent to cover interests in tranquillity and quiet. A noise ordinance, for example, is a proper means to address that interest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But some picketing can be regulated in order to preserve the character of the residential neighborhood?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The abuses that go with certain picketing could be regulated. However, an inherent proscription of all picketing —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So picketing can interfere with privacy and with residential character, and to that extent can be regulated?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: What we have in essence is a balance in such a situation. Picketing may have some impact upon residential privacy interests. However, in looking at that analysis, it is important to remember that normally, as in the Keith case, the interest in residential privacy does not extend to forbid activity taking place on a public forum property. Second, it is important to realize --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you indicate that it can in some instances ?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The Court has not applied the analysis of residential privacy to forbid activity in a public forum of the picketing nature.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, has the Court confronted a case where the public forum doctrine has squarely measured against the interest of the homeowner and privacy and quiet?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The closest that this Court has come to that situation was the Carey case. And in the Carey case --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that went off on equal protection grounds.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Well, in the Carey case --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And in that case, we also said that there is a very strong interest, a very strong constitutional recognition, of the right of privacy.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Granted, you are absolutely correct. However, I would also point out that in the Carey case in footnote that this Court indicated that its justification and the reason for its analysis in the Carey case was the presence of a public forum there. And so it would appear that public forum analysis does appropriately apply to residential streets, which are not significantly different in any major way from the streets and sidewalks in other particular areas. Furthermore, getting back to the residential privacy interests, it is important to remember --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And under Justice Stevens' question, this could go on 365 days a year as far as you are concerned?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: I believe that it could go on for a substantial period of time, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: 365 days a year?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: Yes, I think it could. However, that is an issue that again is not precisely before the Court. Because what we have here is an absolute flat ban on all residential picketing irrespective of how long it extends.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I do not understand how you can say that the length has anything to do with it. I mean either it invades the privacy or it does not invade the privacy. Does it not invade the privacy if it only happens one you can say that they do not annoy anybody. So a difference in degree can make a difference in kind. It is no longer an annoying activity. But if this is an invasion of privacy, it is an invasion of privacy if it occurs one hour or 24 hours, is it not?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: We do not believe that -- to a certain extent, there will always be a degree of privacy invasion. But the problem is that what has to be weighed in the balance is this privacy invasion, whatever it may be, vis-a-vis the important interests of the picketers in the First Amendment expression.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right. Now let us talk about that. What is it that you can only do by picketing that you cannot do by some other form of activity, handing out leaflets and parading around, picketing in the sense in which this ordinance uses the term, parading around the whole neighborhood, handing out leaflets, picketing or doing whatever you want in front of the doctor's offices, what is there distinctive about this, is there anything distinctive about this activity except the invasion of this man's privacy?

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: What is distinctive about picketing activity is that by use of signs and by use of perhaps a do that, under this ordinance, it would be prohibited, under the definition that the town has utilized.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The doctor would probably invite them in for a cup of coffee.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: He might, he might, but he could also report them to the police. Anything is possible under this ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If they liked him that much, of course, they would go away if he asked them, I am sure. I mean he would open the door and say, gee, I really like that, but you fellows are invading my privacy. The best to do if you really like me is to be gone.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The key point here though is that the ordinance sweeps so broadly that it prohibits all of these types of activity. And furthermore, the presence of the interest is residential privacy is an interest which must be considered without respect to the content of speech. If the town's real interest is in prevention of embarrassment to Dr. Victoria, then such an interest should be appropriately dealt with in other fashions. But the First Amendment does not seem to protect an interest in avoiding the embarrassment or other emotional harm which may result from this type of activity or from other types of content based activity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The town is willing to let you march around Dr. Victoria's neighborhood with a sign saying Dr. Victoria is an abortionist or whatever harsher language that you want to use. They are not worried about protecting him from criticism. They are willing to let you do that.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: However —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They just do not want you to annoy him in his home.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: The town's ordinance though —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there anything to stop you from picketing his office? No.

turns.name: MCDOWELL
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So he is right.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. McDowell. The case is submitted.
